headline: Nepal's Farmers Get AI Eyes in the Sky to Fight Crop-Raiding Monkeys
meta_description: Researchers in Nepal are testing AI systems to detect macaques raiding crops, achieving 88% accuracy as farmers struggle with mounting losses.
summary_short: Nepali farmers standing guard at dawn against crop-raiding macaques may soon have high-tech help. Researchers are testing AI systems that detect monkeys with 88% accurac…
body:
At dawn in Birta Deurali village in central Nepal, farmers take turns scanning maize fields for movement, beating drums and waving burning sticks to chase away rhesus macaques. It's exhausting work—and it's not always enough.

Sunmaya Lama, a 32-year-old farmer, lost crops worth about $670 over three years. When she approached local government for compensation, she was turned away. "So we just bear the loss ourselves," she said.

The problem extends far beyond one village. A 2026 study found that nearly half the diet of one macaque troop comes from cultivated crops, and about 15% of Nepal's habitable land experiences moderate to high rates of human-wildlife conflict.

Now, technology is stepping in. At Madan Bhandari University of Science and Technology, researcher Progress Jung Thapa has developed an AI system using cameras and lightweight computers to detect macaques in real time and interpret their behavior. Trained on over 4,000 annotated images, the system achieved around 88% accuracy across 28 field sessions.

The distinction matters: a watching monkey gives farmers precious seconds to respond, while one already feeding requires immediate action. The system sends alerts directly to farmers' phones, turning detection into defense.

Though researchers acknowledge the technology isn't yet a complete solution, the numbers suggest the core detection capability is maturing quickly—and for exhausted farmers, that's progress worth protecting.
